sections.heading: Why Security Matters in Android Development
sections.content: Android apps handle sensitive user data — passwords, payment details, health records, private messages — making security a critical responsibility for every developer. A poorly secured app can expose users to data theft, account takeover, man-in-the-middle attacks, and unauthorized access to device resources. Android provides a layered security model: the Linux kernel enforces process isolation, each app runs in its own sandbox, and the platform provides APIs for secure data storage, encrypted communication, and authentication. Following security best practices protects your users, maintains their trust, satisfies Google Play's security requirements, and reduces your legal and compliance risk.

rows.protection: Process isolation — each app runs as a unique Linux user with its own UID
rows.developer_action: No action needed — enforced by the OS

rows.protection: Apps cannot access each other's files or memory by default
rows.developer_action: Never expose private files via world-readable modes; use FileProvider for sharing

rows.protection: Sensitive APIs and data require explicit user-granted permissions
rows.developer_action: Request only necessary permissions; handle denial gracefully

rows.protection: HTTPS enforced by default on API 28+; cleartext traffic blocked
rows.developer_action: Use HTTPS everywhere; configure Network Security Config for certificate pinning

rows.protection: Full-disk and file-based encryption on modern Android devices
rows.developer_action: Use EncryptedSharedPreferences and EncryptedFile for sensitive app data

rows.protection: Hardware-backed key storage — private keys never leave the secure hardware
rows.developer_action: Use Android Keystore for cryptographic key generation and storage

sections.heading: Secure Data Storage
items: Never store sensitive data in plain SharedPreferences — it is stored as plaintext XML on the device

Use EncryptedSharedPreferences from Jetpack Security for encrypted key-value storage: EncryptedSharedPreferences.create(context, "secret_prefs", masterKey, EncryptedSharedPreferences.PrefKeyEncryptionScheme.AES256_SIV, EncryptedSharedPreferences.PrefValueEncryptionScheme.AES256_GCM)
Use EncryptedFile for encrypting files at rest: EncryptedFile.Builder(context, file, masterKey, EncryptedFile.FileEncryptionScheme.AES256_GCM_HKDF_4KB).build()
Generate a MasterKey using the Keystore-backed AES256_GCM spec: MasterKey.Builder(context).setKeyScheme(MasterKey.KeyScheme.AES256_GCM).build()
Never store passwords in plaintext — store only salted hashes or use Android Keystore to store tokens
Do not store sensitive data in external storage — it is accessible to other apps and the user
Avoid logging sensitive data — strip all PII from Logcat logs before release
Use Android Keystore to store cryptographic keys — keys are hardware-backed and never exported

rows.purpose: Generates or retrieves an AES-256 master key stored in the Android Keystore
SharedPreferences implementation that encrypts both keys and values at rest
Reads and writes files with AES-256-GCM encryption transparently

sections.heading: Network Security
sections.content: All network communication in your app should use HTTPS with TLS to prevent man-in-the-middle attacks and data interception. Since Android 9 (API 28), cleartext HTTP traffic is blocked by default — apps must explicitly opt in to allow it. The Network Security Configuration file (res/xml/network_security_config.xml) gives you declarative control over trust anchors, cleartext permissions, and certificate pinning without changing any code. Certificate pinning adds an extra layer of security by ensuring your app only trusts specific certificates or public keys, preventing attacks even if a certificate authority is compromised.

sections.heading: Network Security Configuration
items: Declare in AndroidManifest.xml: android:networkSecurityConfig="@xml/network_security_config"
Block all cleartext traffic (default on API 28+): <base-config cleartextTrafficPermitted="false" />
Allow cleartext only for specific domains during development: <domain-config cleartextTrafficPermitted="true"><domain includeSubdomains="true">dev.example.com</domain></domain-config>
Add custom trust anchors for debug builds to trust user-installed certificates: <debug-overrides><trust-anchors><certificates src="user" /></trust-anchors></debug-overrides>
Certificate pinning — pin a specific certificate: <pin-set><pin digest="SHA-256">base64encodedHash==</pin></pin-set>
Always pin at least two certificates (primary + backup) to avoid being locked out during cert rotation
Generate pin hash: openssl x509 -in cert.pem -pubkey -noout | openssl pkey -pubin -outform der | openssl dgst -sha256 -binary | base64
Test network security config with: adb shell cmd connectivity airplane-mode enable to simulate offline

rows.reason: Encrypts data in transit — prevents eavesdropping and tampering
rows.implementation: All API base URLs must use https:// — enforce in Retrofit base URL

rows.reason: Default OkHttp validates certificates against system trust store
rows.implementation: Never use custom TrustManager that accepts all certificates — this disables SSL validation entirely

rows.reason: Prevents MITM attacks even if a CA is compromised
rows.implementation: Use Network Security Config or OkHttp CertificatePinner

rows.reason: Programmatic pinning with fallback pin support
Older TLS versions have known vulnerabilities
rows.implementation: Modern Android (API 29+) uses TLS 1.3 by default — OkHttp enforces modern cipher suites

sections.heading: Android Keystore System
items: Android Keystore stores cryptographic keys in secure hardware (TEE or StrongBox) — keys never leave the secure enclave
Generate a key pair in Keystore: val keyPairGenerator = KeyPairGenerator.getInstance(KeyProperties.KEY_ALGORITHM_EC, "AndroidKeyStore")
Specify key properties: KeyGenParameterSpec.Builder(alias, KeyProperties.PURPOSE_SIGN or KeyProperties.PURPOSE_VERIFY).setDigests(KeyProperties.DIGEST_SHA256).build()
Require user authentication to use the key: .setUserAuthenticationRequired(true).setUserAuthenticationValidityDurationSeconds(30)
Use setStrongBoxBacked(true) on supported devices to store keys in dedicated security chip (API 28+)
Retrieve stored key: KeyStore.getInstance("AndroidKeyStore").apply { load(null) }.getEntry(alias, null) as KeyStore.PrivateKeyEntry
Keys generated in Keystore cannot be exported — they can only be used for cryptographic operations via the Keystore API
Use Keystore for: signing tokens, encrypting/decrypting data, biometric-gated operations

rows.description: System-provided biometric authentication UI — fingerprint, face, or iris depending on device
Check if biometric authentication is available and enrolled before showing the prompt
Tie Keystore key usage to biometric authentication — key only usable after successful biometric verification
rows.implementation: Pass CryptoObject(cipher) to BiometricPrompt.authenticate() — cipher initialized with Keystore key

rows.description: Allow PIN/pattern/password as fallback if biometrics fail or are not enrolled
Store auth tokens in EncryptedSharedPreferences — never in plain SharedPreferences or plain files
rows.implementation: Use EncryptedSharedPreferences with a Keystore-backed MasterKey

sections.heading: Protecting Against Common Vulnerabilities
items: SQL Injection — always use Room's @Query with parameter binding; never concatenate user input into raw SQL strings
Content Provider exposure — set android:exported="false" on ContentProviders not intended for other apps; use permissions to restrict access
Intent hijacking — use explicit Intents when starting your own components; never use implicit Intents for sensitive actions
Deep link validation — validate all incoming deep link parameters before use; never trust URL parameters blindly
Exported Activity exposure — set android:exported="false" on Activities not meant to be launched by other apps
WebView security — disable JavaScript if not needed: webView.settings.javaScriptEnabled = false; never load arbitrary URLs from untrusted sources
WebView file access — disable file access: webView.settings.allowFileAccess = false; webView.settings.allowContentAccess = false
Backup exposure — sensitive data may be included in Android backups; use android:allowBackup="false" or exclude sensitive files with backup rules

rows.recommendation: Disable unless your app explicitly requires JavaScript — enables XSS attack surface
Always disable — prevents WebView from reading local files via file:// URIs
Disable unless needed — prevents access to ContentProvider URIs from WebView
Annotate exposed methods with @JavascriptInterface; only expose to trusted content; never expose to arbitrary URLs
Override shouldOverrideUrlLoading() to validate URLs before loading — reject unexpected schemes
rows.code: override fun shouldOverrideUrlLoading(view: WebView, request: WebResourceRequest): Boolean { return !request.url.host.endsWith("example.com") }

sections.heading: Securing App Components
items: Set android:exported="false" on all Activities, Services, BroadcastReceivers, and ContentProviders not intentionally public
From API 31+, android:exported must be explicitly declared for components with intent filters — build will fail otherwise
Use custom permissions to restrict access to your exported components: android:permission="com.example.MY_PERMISSION"
Validate all data received via Intents — never trust Intent extras from external sources without validation
Use PendingIntent.FLAG_IMMUTABLE for all PendingIntents (API 31+ requirement) to prevent PendingIntent hijacking
For BroadcastReceivers handling sensitive actions, use LocalBroadcastManager or specify a permission in sendBroadcast()
Avoid storing sensitive data in Intent extras passed between apps — other apps may intercept them
Use signature-level permissions for inter-app communication between your own apps

rows.description: Shrinks, obfuscates, and optimizes code — makes reverse engineering significantly harder
Renames classes, methods, and fields to meaningless names — harder to reverse engineer
rows.implementation: Enabled automatically with isMinifyEnabled = true and R8

rows.description: Removes unused resources from the APK — reduces attack surface and APK size
Preserve classes that must not be obfuscated — data classes, Retrofit interfaces, Room entities
Verify obfuscation worked by inspecting the release APK
rows.implementation: Android Studio > Build > Analyze APK — inspect classes.dex to verify obfuscation

sections.heading: Sensitive Data — Do's and Don'ts
items: DO: Use EncryptedSharedPreferences for tokens, API keys, and user preferences
DO: Use Android Keystore for cryptographic keys — never store raw keys in SharedPreferences or files
DO: Use HTTPS for all network communication — no exceptions
DO: Validate and sanitize all input from external sources — Intents, deep links, WebView, user input
DO: Obfuscate your release builds with R8/ProGuard
DON'T: Hardcode API keys, secrets, or credentials in source code or string resources — they are visible in the APK
DON'T: Log sensitive data — strip PII and tokens from all Logcat output before release
DON'T: Store sensitive data in external storage, SharedPreferences without encryption, or SQLite without encryption
DON'T: Trust data received via Intent extras, deep link parameters, or WebView without validation
DON'T: Use android:debuggable="true" in release builds — always ensure it is false or absent in production

rows.description: All API calls use HTTPS; cleartext traffic disabled in Network Security Config
No API keys, tokens, or passwords in source code, strings.xml, or BuildConfig fields
EncryptedSharedPreferences and EncryptedFile used for all sensitive data at rest
isMinifyEnabled = true for release builds; proguard-rules verified
Release build is not debuggable — no debug flag in release buildType
android:exported explicitly set on all components; unexported components set to false
All Log.d/Log.e calls audited — no tokens, passwords, or PII in Logcat output
android:allowBackup set appropriately; sensitive files excluded from backup
All PendingIntents use FLAG_IMMUTABLE (API 31+ requirement)
JavaScript disabled unless required; file access disabled; URL validation in shouldOverrideUrlLoading()

sections.heading: Summary
sections.content: Android security is a multi-layered discipline covering secure storage, encrypted networking, proper component exposure, and code obfuscation. Use EncryptedSharedPreferences and EncryptedFile for sensitive data at rest, Android Keystore for cryptographic keys, and HTTPS with certificate pinning for all network communication. Lock down app components by setting android:exported="false" on private components, validate all external input from Intents and deep links, and harden WebViews by disabling JavaScript and file access where not needed. Enable R8/ProGuard obfuscation in release builds, strip all sensitive data from logs, and never hardcode secrets in source code. Run through the security checklist before every release to ensure your app meets both user expectations and Google Play's security requirements.
